coredata.dc:title: In-situ formation of dual-gradient hydrogels through microfluidic mixing and co-extrusion for constructing an engineered antibacterial platform
coredata.prism:publicationName: Composites Part A: Applied Science and Manufacturing

Niu, Chunhua

Liang, Xuexue
Gu, Wanjun
Wei, Zhong
Chen, Kai
Bohinc, Klemen
Guo, Xuhong
coredata.dc:description: The explosive release of antibiotics caused by drug delivery hydrogels can potentially cause cell damage. To balance the antibacterial activity and cytotoxicity of drug-loaded hydrogels, we present a double gradient hydrogel (PCHH) using microfluidic mixing co-extrusion technology. The gradient structure of the hydrogel can be controlled by adjusting the distribution of sodium alginate and amikacin. This allows for different swelling and drug release behaviors which can maintain a wet environment and optimize drug release behaviors. Amikacin is released at a rate of 50 % within 24 h and achieves sustained and uniform drug release in the later stages. The antibacterial properties displays that the synergistic antibacterial activity between amikacin and carbon dots reduces the dosage of amikacin and decreases bacterial resistance. This design principle offers new opportunities for hydrogels engineered with microfluidic chemistry, which can be applied in tissue engineering, soft robotics, and active medical implants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A-Layered structures
A-Smart materials
E-Extrusion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of dual gradient hierarchically sustained release hydrogel (PCHH)
The morphological structure of double-gradient hydrogels
Drug release behavior of double gradient hydrogels
Antibacterial testing of double gradient hydrogels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the fabrication of dual-gradient PCHH-n hydrogels by MMCE and the mechanism of synergistic antibacterial process.

A) TEM images, B) particle size distribution, and C) UV–vis absorbance spectra of CD (The inserted part was photographs of CD aqueous solution in sunlight and 365 nm UV excitation). D, E) FT-IR spectrum of the DEX, ODEX, AMK, PCHH-0B hydrogel, and PCHH-0T hydrogel. F) XPS spectrum of CD/ODEX, CD/ODEX/AMK, CD/ODEX/SA/AMK hydrogel, and corresponding high-resolution XPS of G) C 1s, H) N 1s, and I) O 1s.

SEM image of A, B) PCHH-0B hydrogels (B represents the bottom hydrogel); C, D) PCHH-0T hydrogels (T represents the top hydrogel), E, G, H, I) PCHH-n (n = 0, 1, 3, 5) hydrogels. F) Schematic representation of double-layer hydrogel network.

A) Possible mechanism of drug release from hydrogels. B, C) UV–vis absorption spectra of derivatives of amikacin at different concentrations and standard curve. Degradation behavior of PCHH-n (n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5) hydrogels at D) pH 7.4 and E) pH 5.5. Cumulative drug release of amikacin in the media of F) pH 7.4, G) pH 5.5.

Antimicrobial activity in vitro. A) The contact Antibacterial of control, ODEX, CD, AMK, and PCHH-3 hydrogel for 10 min and B) Photograph of inhibition zone of the PCHH-n hydrogel against S. aureus and E. coli.

A) Inhibition zone of the control group and PCHH-n hydrogel against E. coli and S. aureus and B) the relative bacterial viability after treating with PBS, ODEX, CD, AMK, PCHH-3 hydrogel discs for 10 min. The SEM images of C) E. coli and D) S. aureus samples treated by (1) PBS and (2) PCHH-3 hydrogel. Scale bar = 1 µm. E) Antibacterial mechanism of PCHH-n hydrogel.

The synergism between amikacin and carbon dots was investigated by checkerboard assay (A). Checkerboard dilution assays performed on S. aureus, which was used to evaluate the synergy between amikacin and carbon dots (B). Drug resistance development profiles of S. aureus (SHBCC D10842) during serial passaging in the presence of sub-MIC dosage of free amikacin, CD, or the combination of CD and AMK. The y-axis indicates the folds of increased MIC compared to the initial ones (C). The growth curve of S. aureus strains in the presence of amikacin and/or carbon dots (D, E).

head.ce:title: In-situ formation of dual-gradient hydrogels through microfluidic mixing and co-extrusion for constructing an engineered antibacterial platform

School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The explosive release of antibiotics caused by drug delivery hydrogels can potentially cause cell damage. To balance the antibacterial activity and cytotoxicity of drug-loaded hydrogels, we present a double gradient hydrogel (PCHH) using microfluidic mixing co-extrusion technology. The gradient structure of the hydrogel can be controlled by adjusting the distribution of sodium alginate and amikacin. This allows for different swelling and drug release behaviors which can maintain a wet environment and optimize drug release behaviors. Amikacin is released at a rate of 50 % within 24 h and achieves sustained and uniform drug release in the later stages. The antibacterial properties displays that the synergistic antibacterial activity between amikacin and carbon dots reduces the dosage of amikacin and decreases bacterial resistance. This design principle offers new opportunities for hydrogels engineered with microfluidic chemistry, which can be applied in tissue engineering, soft robotics, and active medical implants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are effective in treating bacterial infections. However, the inappropriate use of antibiotics has led to antimicrobial resistance (AMR) [1], and further increases morbidity and mortality [2,3]. Researchers are actively seeking strategies to enhance the antibacterial activity of existing antibiotics and improve bacteria's sensitivity to antibiotics through targeted delivery using functionalized nanoparticles [4], coatings [5], and hydrogels as drug delivery platforms [6–8]. Polysaccharide-based Schiff base hydrogels are a promising delivery platform due to their pH responsiveness, ease of functionalization, and excellent biocompatibility [9,10]. Hydrogel delivery platforms are widely using the synergistic antibacterial strategy to avoid bacterial resistance and antibiotic overuse [11]. These platforms deliver antibiotics, such as amikacin (AMK) [5], ciprofloxacin [12], and cefotaxime [13], along with other materials that have antibacterial activity, such as amino-functionalized carbon dots (CD)[14] and antimicrobial peptides (AMP) [13]. According to Li et al., tobramycin was immobilized in antibacterial coatings through a Schiff base reaction and slowly released under slightly acidic conditions [5]. Similarly, ceftazidime and AMP can be simultaneously released from Schiff base hydrogels to promote synergistic antibacterial activity [13]. These hydrogels are also widely used in tissue engineering, nerve repair, and tissue adhesion [15]. However, the development of hydrogels is hindered by the explosive release of drugs and subsequent inadequate drug concentration, which can lead to secondary infections [16]. Therefore, the challenge is to design advanced Schiff base hydrogels that integrate anti-explosive release and reduce AMR [1].

Interestingly, layered hydrogels have the potential to achieve uniform and slow drug release by adjusting the composition of each layer, making them a promising option for drug delivery platforms [17]. This has garnered significant attention in the field. Wang et al. prepared a double-layer hydrogel that achieved the sequential release of active substances [18]. Similarly, Mooney et al. prepared a double-layer hydrogel through UV polymerization and studied the release process of corticosteroids [19]. However, the preparation of multi-layer hydrogels is often complicated [20], time-consuming, and requires additional crosslinking agents (glutaraldehyde, succinic anhydride) [21], as well as energy input [22], which can result in environmental pollution and material waste [23,24]. Additionally, Guo et al. developed multi-layer-controlled release materials using a multiplier and extruder [25,26]. And it requires melt extrusion of the polymer, which involves complex and expensive equipment and heat input. Christman et al. achieved rapid gelation of functionalized polyethylene glycol through rapid mixing of materials [27], while the structure and function of hydrogels cannot be further modulated. Compared to the methods mentioned above, microfluidic chemistry presents a promising approach for achieving rapid mixing and reactions of materials [28], as well as preparing delivery materials [29,30]. The combined fluid mixing and extruder enables the in situ gelation and structure modulation of hydrogels, as well as the preparation of functionalized double-layer hydrogels based on a Schiff base reaction. However, there are few reports on the preparation of double-layer hydrogels using microfluidic mixing and co-extrusion (MMCE) technology.

Inspired by this, we fabricated the MMCE devices utilizing 3D printing. The MMCE devices are capable of expeditious and continuous preparation of bilayer hydrogels based on the rapid mixing of solutions and in situ gelation, obviating the necessity for additional energy inputs and complex and expensive equipment. By modulating the distribution of key components in the hydrogels, a series of dual-gradient hydrogels (PCHH hydrogels) with a pore size gradient and a drug concentration gradient were developed and probed for their drug release behavior and antimicrobial activity. The pore size gradient can improve the swelling behavior of hydrogels. Furthermore, the modulation of drug distribution in the hydrogel can significantly improve the drug release behavior. The reduction of the drug concentration in the bottom layer can prevent the explosive release of the drug and result in a sustained, homogeneous release of the drug. The combination of CD and AMK released from the PCHH hydrogel during degradation has a synergistic antimicrobial effect, which not only significantly reduces the amount of antibiotics but also prevents the development of drug resistance in bacteria. The MMCE platform enables the continuous and rapid preparation of hydrogels with a wide range of applications in drug delivery, tissue engineering, and sensing. This provides a promising approach for functional tailoring and scale-up preparation of hydrogels.

Dextran (Mw ∼ 70000), and Sodium Periodate (99.5 %) were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical (China). ε-Poly-L-Lysine (Mw = 3500–4600, 99 %) and sodium alginate (SA, 500–1000 mPa·s) were purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific (China). Amikacin Sulfate (674 ∼ 786 μg/mg) and PBS buffer were provided by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology (China). 3D printing resin was purchased from Formlabs (American). Luria–Bertani (LB) broth medium and LB agar medium were purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific (China). All reagents were used directly without further purification.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of CD was obtained with a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd, Japan) to observe the morphology and particle size distribution of CD. HRTEM with mapping was used to observe the element distribution of antibacterial hydrogel. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to observe the morphology of the hydrogel. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) of antibacterial hydrogel was carried out with a 300 W Al-Kα radiation model electron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi, VG Scientific)) to analyze the elemental composition. Fourier transformed-infrared spectra (FT-IR) of AMK, DEX, ODEX, and antibacterial hydrogels were observed with a spectrograph (Nicolet 380) to analyze functional group changes during stepwise preparation of the material, Ultraviolet–vis absorption of CD was used with a spectrophotometer (UV-2600) and a fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer-LS55) respectively to demonstrate the characteristic absorption peaks of CD.

The same mass of dextran and sodium periodate (3 g) was dissolved in 50 mL PBS solution, the mixed solution was stirred for 12 h in a dark environment at room temperature. Ethylene glycol with an equimolar molar mass of sodium periodate was added and stirred for 4 h to terminate the reaction. The obtained orange-yellow solution was removed into a dialysis bag (14000 Da) for 5 days. ODEX was obtained after freeze-drying, and the degree of oxidation (DO) was determined by hydroxylamine hydrochloride-potentiometric titration.

An appropriate amount of ε-Poly-L-Lysine (1 g) was calcined in air at 240 °C for 3 h, the resulting bright yellow powder was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, sonicated for 1 h, and centrifuged (10000 rpm for 8 min). The obtained orange-yellow supernatant was dialyzed (5000 Da) for 5 days and CD was obtained after freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dual gradient hierarchically sustained release hydrogel (PCHH)
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of the MMCE device: The MMCE device was printed from a UV-curable resin (Clear Resin V4, Formlabs Inc., America) using a desktop SLA 3D printer (Form 3, America). The printer employs an inverted stereolithographic process wherein the print is built from bottom up, the laser being directed from below the resin tank and the build head rising an increment after each cured layer. The 250 mW laser has a wavelength of 405 nm. The print layer height in the z-direction was set to 25 µm.

A Y-junction microfluidic device was fabricated using a light-curing 3D printer (Form 3, America). The device has 2 inlet ports which are connected to a rectangular channel with a height of 1 mm, the co-flow region is 10 mm in length; two devices meet at the Y-junction region which has a total height of 2 mm. The co-flow region is 10 mm in length. By adjusting the number of reaction units, the size of multi-layer hydrogels was controlled around 1 cm wide and thickness of 2 mm. More details are provided in the Supporting Information, Figure S1.

The Preparation of PCHH: the PCHH was prepared according to the following method in sequence within the MMCE device. First, 500 mg ODEX and 100 mg SA were dissolved in 5 mL ultrapure water to prepare a precursor solution as solution 1. Then 250 mg of CD was dissolved in 5 mL ultrapure water with different amounts of AMK as solution 2. The top layer of PCHH was formed by mixing solution 1 and solution 2. Similarly, the bottom layer was prepared in the same way without SA in solution 1. The total drug loading (0.9 mg/mL) was maintained, and the drug loading of the bottom layer was regulated. Solution 1 and solution 2 were injected into the MMCE device with a stable flow rate (8 mL/min). A variety of PCHH can be obtained, named PCHH-nm (n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. n represent the ratio of drug loading of the bottom layer hydrogel to the total drug loading, e.g., 1 represents 10 %; m = B, T. B represent the bottom hydrogel, T represent the top hydrogel.). According to the Gao’s method [31], the prepared PCHH hydrogels were freeze-dried using a conventional freezer dryer at − 90 °C for 48 h in a vacuum chamber to eliminate water from the samples, and then SEM was used to observe the morphology and structure of the hydrogels.

PCHH-n with a diameter of 1 cm was prepared by a puncher and placed in an EP tube with the same diameter (One hundred microliters of hydrogels, 3 mg CD, and 300 μg AMK). Then acetic acid-sodium acetate buffer with different pH values (pH 7.4 or pH 5.5) was added. The release behavior of AMK was evaluated by a well-established derivatization method using ninhydrin. To prepare the derivatization reagent, 85 mg ninhydrin and 15 mg reduced ninhydrin were dissolved in 10 mL ethylene glycol methyl ether, and the derivatization reagent was freshly prepared before measurement. The EP tube was incubated at 37 °C, and 1 mL sample was taken after dialysis. Then 1 ml dialysate was fully mixed with 500 µL derivatization reagent and 500 µL acetic acid-sodium acetate buffer (pH = 5.0) at scheduled time points, and an equal volume of fresh acetic acid-sodium acetate buffer was replenished. The mixture was then heated to 100 °C for 10 min and then cooled to room temperature. During this process, the color of the reaction system would change from brownish yellow to violet. The absorbance of the samples at 570 nm was recorded for colorimetric analysis. UV–vis spectroscopy characterization was carried out by a PerkinElmer Lambda 35 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Three repeats were conducted for each sample.

Inhibition zone: The PCHH-n hydrogels were prepared as a disc with a diameter of 0.8 cm and a thickness of 2 mm and transferred to an LB agar plate covered with 100 μL of 1 × 107 CFU/mL fresh bacterial suspension. After incubating at 37 °C overnight, the bacterial inhibition zone was measured. Each experiment was repeated three times. Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) SHBCC D10842 and Escherichia coli (E. coli) ATCC 35218 purchased from Shanghai Bioresource Collection Center were chosen as the model pathogen for antibacterial activity experiments. Before the experiments, S. aureus and E. coli were cultivated in Luria–Bertani (LB) nutrient broth at 37 °C for 16h and determined its concentration.

Contact antibacterial: the prepared PCHH-n hydrogels were placed into a 96-well plate, respectively. Then 100 μL of 1 × 106 CFU/mL fresh S. aureus suspension was added to each well. After incubating at 37 °C for 10 min in a relatively humidified atmosphere, 1 mL of sterile PBS was added to each well to resuspend the surviving bacteria with a moderate sonication (f = 50 Hz). 100 μL of suspension was covered with an LB agar plate. The results were observed after incubation for 16h at 37 °C. 20 μL of 1 × 107 CFU/mL S. aureus suspension was dropped into the well without hydrogels and was treated as a control. Count the number of colonies on the agar plates and calculate the killing ratios against S. aureus and E. coli. Each experiment was repeated three times.

Determination of minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC): The MIC of the different treatments on various bacteria strains was determined [32]. Briefly, broth media (100 μL) containing two-fold serial dilutions of each compound were placed into a 96-well tissue culture plate. Each bacterial strain was taken from an exponentially growing culture and diluted to 1 × 106 CFU/mL. Ten microliters of such microbial suspension were inoculated in each well of the 5 plates, resulting in a final bacteria concentration of approximately 1 × 105 CFU/mL. The bacteria were cultured at 37 °C for one day and their growth was observed by reading the optical density (OD) at 600 nm. The MIC was determined as the treatment concentration at which no microbial growth was observed. The microbial suspension (100 μL, 1 × 106 CFU/mL) containing two-fold serial dilutions of each compound (CD, AMK) incubating at 37 °C for 60 min in a relatively humidified atmosphere. Then 100 μL of suspension was transferred to an LB agar plate. The results were observed after incubation for 16h at 37 °C. The MBC was determined as the treatment concentration at which no microbial colonies were observed. Each experiment was repeated three times. Broth containing microbial cells alone was used as a positive control and broth without bacteria inoculation was used as a negative control. Each test was carried out in three replicates.

Fractional inhibitory concentration index (FICI) measurement: The synergy between the cationic polymer and the antibiotic was assessed by checkerboard assays. The two antimicrobial components (CD, AMK) were mixed in a 96-well plate with serial two-dimensional dilutions. Bacteria were inoculated in each well of the plate and their growth, reflected by the OD600 value, was monitored. According to the Gong’s method [1], the synergy effect was evaluated by calculating the FIC index (FICI) according to the formula below:
FICI =
M a
M A
+
M b
M B

MA and MB indicate the MICs of the free antibiotic and carbon dots. Ma and Mb indicate the MICs of the two components in combination. The interactions between the two components are defined according to standard criteria of considering FICI ≤ 0.5 as synergistic; 0.5 < FICI ≤ 1 as additive; 1 < FICI ≤ 4 as indifference; and FICI > 4 as antagonism.

Drug resistance development measurement: S. aureus SHBCC D10842 was employed to study drug resistance development under sublethal dose treatments of free amikacin or the combination of amikacin and carbon dots. Bacteria were seeded in the EP tubes containing 1 mL media (1 × 106 CFU/tube) with a serially diluted free antibiotic the combination of free antibiotic and carbon dots and passaged every 24 h. The OD600 value in each tube was examined to determine the MIC and the bacteria cultured in the tube containing rifampicin at a ½ MIC was used for passage. The MIC for each passage was recorded and compared to that of the first passage to determine the drug resistance enhancement.

According to previous studies [31], the cytotoxicity tests of hydrogels were conducted using NIH-3 T3 cell which provided by Shanghai Quicell Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Briefly, 1 g PCHH-3 hydrogel or traditionally-made hydrogel was rinsed with 5 mL complete DMEM medium containing 10 wt% FBS and 1 wt% penicillin − streptomycin, soaked in 15 mL medium for 24 h in an incubator. The relative concentration of the hydrogel extract (1 g/15 mL) is defined as 100 %. 100 μL of NIH-3 T3 cell suspension (passage number: 4) was added to a 96-well plate (5 × 104 cells per well) and incubated at 37 °C under 5 % CO2. Cells on the 96-well plate with traditional-made hydrogel were used as control. The cells were then incubated for 24 h, 48 h and 96 h. The culture medium was removed. Each well was washed by PBS three times, further incubated in the incubator for 4 h. The supernatant was removed and 100 μL DMSO was added, shaked gently for 10 min, and measured for the absorption at 450 nm. A Live/Dead Viability/Cytotoxicity kit (Molecular Probe) was used to stain the NIH-3 T3 cells. First, the cells were incubated in PBS containing 2 μM Calcein AM and 1 μM ethidium homodimer-1 (EthD-1) for 30 min in a 96-well cell culture plate. Live cells were stained with Calcein AM (green), and dead cells were stained with EthD-1 (red). The cells were then incubated for 24 h, 48 h and 96 h and imaged for morphology with a fluorescence microscope.

In this work, MMCE devices were made using 3D printing. Subsequently, the MMCE devices were used to prepare PCHH hydrogels based on the self-healing and injectable ability of Schiff base hydrogels, as shown in Fig. 1 . In comparison to the melt extrusion process [25,26], the MMCE devices offer a novel approach to the preparation of multilayer hydrogels which avoids the additional heat inputs and complex instruments and equipment. And the MMCE device is able to integrate the in-situ moulding of bilayer hydrogels and the modulation of the gradient structures for the preparation of multifunctional hydrogels, compared to Christman's work [27]. The distribution of sodium alginate in the hydrogel affects the formation of hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions, thereby resulting in the formation of a pore size gradient in the hydrogel. This modification facilitates the absorption of exudate and helps maintain a moist environment. The drug concentration gradient inside the hydrogel can be altered by adjusting the distribution of drugs at the top and bottom of the hydrogel, which affects the drug release behavior. This homemade MMCE device is able to prepare bi-gradient hydrogels quickly and continuously, which reduces tedious steps [22], avoids the use of toxic and hazardous chemical raw materials [20], and achieves environmentally green and sustainable development, as shown in Table S4. AMK, an aminoglycoside antibiotic, was selected as a model drug due to its excellent antibacterial activity [33]. During the antibacterial process, AMK and CD can be released gradually to achieve synergistic antibacterial effects [34].

The structural properties of carbon dots including the morphology, size distribution, and optical characteristics, were further characterized by TEM and UV–vis absorbance spectra. The CD has a particle size of 7.67 nm and a quasi-circular structure, with absorption peaks at 207 nm and 261 nm, as shown in Fig. 2 A-C. Furthermore, the colorless CD solution showed bright blue fluorescence under UV excitation, which proved that the CD was successfully prepared with fluorescent properties. As shown in Fig. 2D, by comparing the FTIR spectra of ODEX with dextran (DEX), DEX has stronger hydroxyl absorption peaks (3200–3600 cm−1), whereas the characteristic signal at 1730 cm−1 suggests that the hydroxyl group of DEX is oxidized to an aldehyde group, which is further used as a chemical binding site [35]. Fig. 2E shows that the absorption peak (1730 cm−1) in the spectra of PCHH-0B and PCHH-0T hydrogels decreased or disappeared. Additionally, a new absorption peak at 1676 cm−1 was observed, which was attributed to the formation of Schiff base bonds between the aldehyde group of ODEX and the amino groups of CD and AMK. The absorption peak (1510–1580 cm−1) in PCHH-0T hydrogels was similar to that of PCHH-0B hydrogels, indicating successful doping of AMK into the hydrogel.

The chemical structure of PCHH hydrogel was analyzed through XPS. Fig. 2F displays the C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s peaks of PCHH hydrogel, which correspond to 284.6 eV, 399.4 eV, and 532.5 eV, respectively. The C 1 s peak was deconvoluted, revealing peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.9 eV, and 287.5 eV, corresponding to C–C/C–H, C-N/C-O, and C = O [36], as shown in Fig. 2G. Compared to CD/ODEX, the addition of AMK in CD/ODEX/AMK hydrogel resulted in a decrease in the C = O bond, indicating the consumption of some aldehyde groups by AMK. In contrast, the surface of SA is rich in hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which enhances non-covalent interactions between carboxyl, amino, and aldehyde groups within the hydrogel, leading to an increase in the C-O and C = O peaks. Fig. 2H shows that the N element analysis revealed peaks at 399.4 eV, 401.2 eV, and 401.7 eV, corresponding to C = C-N, C = N, and N–H. The C = N peak in CD/ODEX/AMK is higher than in CD/ODEX, which is attributed to the formation of more imine groups due to the addition of AMK. The addition of SA improved the hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions between aldehyde, amino, and carboxyl groups in the PCHH hydrogel, thus affecting the formation of Schiff base bonds. In Fig. 2I, the peaks of the O element are 531.2 eV (C = O) and 532.5 eV (C-O) [37,38]. Compared to DEX, SA surfaces have not only hydroxyl groups but also carboxyl groups, leading to an increase in C = O groups and oxygen content (Table S2). Additionally, the elemental distribution analysis (Fig. S2) indicates a uniform distribution of C, N, and O elements throughout the hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphological structure of double-gradient hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: In order to consolidate the polymer network structure formed by covalent and non-covalent interactions and to improve drug release behavior, a controlled chemical cross-linking step is essential, in addition to physical interactions [14]. A microfluidic mixing and co-extrusion reactors (MMCE) were prepared via 3D printing, as shown in Fig. S1. The hydrogels exhibit excellent injectability and self-healing capability due to the dynamic reversible crosslinking of Schiff base hydrogels. Dual-gradient PCHH-n hydrogels were constructed by rapid mixing of solutions and in situ ultrafast gelation in the MMCE devices. The distribution of SA and AMK in the hydrogel was controlled to regulate the gradient structure. A series of engineered hydrogels were prepared by the Schiff base reaction of aldehyde-functionalized ODEX and amino-functionalized CD. These hydrogels were then utilized to load amikacin, an aminoglycoside antibiotic, as demonstrated in Table S1. The hydrogels can rapidly self-heal within 20 s, as shown in Figure S5.

Fig. 3 F demonstrates a clear interface between the PCHH-0B and PCHH-0T hydrogels, both of which can gel rapidly within 20 s (Figure S3). Furthermore, after freeze-drying, both PCHH-0B and PCHH-0T hydrogels exhibit a typical 3D porous morphology in cross-section [39]. The hydrogels exhibited a distinct gradient in pore size, as illustrated in Fig. 3C and D. The top hydrogel had a narrower pore size distribution (68 ± 12 μm) compared to the bottom hydrogel (96 ± 23 μm). This was attributed to the enhanced electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding between groups such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxylic acid groups in the hydrogel network due to the addition of SA (Fig. 3A and B). Additionally, the gelation time was reduced (Figure. S3). The swelling curves of PCHH-3 hydrogels indicate that the top layer reaches swelling equilibrium within 200 min (17.4 g/g), while the bottom layer reaches it faster (120 min) and absorbs more solution (25.8 g/g). This pore-size gradient has a capillary effect that promotes the absorption of the leachate (Fig. S6) [40]. The pore size gradient of the PCHH-n (n = 0, 1, 3, 5) hydrogels remained unchanged despite varying the amount of AMK. Additionally, the pore sizes did not show significant variation (Fig. 3E, G-I). This indicates that slight changes in the amount of AMK did not affect the pore size distribution of the PCHH-n hydrogels. The MMCE enables the rapid in-situ moulding and continuous preparation of hydrogels in comparison to complex 3D printing devices or casting using elaborate micro-moulds [41,42] (Fig. S4). The MMCE device can be used to obtain functionalized hydrogels, which has promising applications in sensors and tissue engineering [43,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug release behavior of double gradient hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para:
The custom optimization process of AMK release behavior was further analyzed by evaluating the degradation and drug release behavior of PCHH-n hydrogel in neutral and slightly acidic environments (Fig. S9). Fig. 4
A shows that the PCHH-n hydrogel, prepared by the Schiff base reaction, exhibits intrinsic pH-responsive characteristics. Furthermore, the degradation curves of PCHH-n hydrogel are similar under neutral and slightly acidic conditions. This similarity is mainly due to the fact that the small amount of AMK added does not affect the network structure of hydrogels. The remaining mass of the hydrogel is approximately 89.6 % under neutral conditions and 19.3 % under mildly acidic conditions as shown in Fig. 4D and E. The drug release behavior in PCHH-n hydrogel was evaluated using established ninhydrin-derived reagents (Fig. 4B and C). After the combination of amikacin and ninhydrin-derived reagents, significant absorption peaks at 570 nm were observed. A standard curve was obtained based on the antibiotic concentration within a certain range of concentrations. The custom-designed dual gradient hydrogel provides tailored drug release via covalent and noncovalent interactions between ODEX, SA, CD and AMK [45]. Fig. 4F illustrates that the release amount of PCHH-n (n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5) hydrogels ranges from 7 % to 18 % under neutral conditions. For PCHH-0, there is still a cumulative drug release of 3 %, indicating that after the hydrogel reaches swelling equilibrium, the AMK bound by non-covalent interactions is readily released from the interior of the hydrogel by free diffusion and permeation.

The drug release behavior during non-linear degradation of PCHH-n hydrogels can be tuned by modulating drug distribution in hydrogels (Fig. 4G, F). It is important to note that in the initial stage of release (0–24 h), the cumulative drug release reached a turning point (Fig. S7 A). This was caused by the layer-by-layer disintegration of hydrogel due to the dissociation of Schiff base bonds, electrostatic interactions, and hydrogen bonding in the PCHH-n hydrogels [33]. The drug in the bottom hydrogel is almost completely released within 24 h., but the bottom hydrogel has not yet fully degraded. Diffusion of AMK from the top hydrogel to the bottom and its release requires diffusion through the hydrogel network. As the drug content in the top hydrogel decreases from PCHH-1 hydrogel to PCHH-5 hydrogel, the cumulative drug release gradually slows down from 24 to 96 h. (Fig. S7B). Compared to other hydrogels, PCHH-3 releases only 14 % of the drug under neutral conditions (pH = 7.4) (Fig. 4F). However, under acidic conditions, 50 % of the drug is released within 24 h., indicating rapid sterilization in the early stages of bacterial infection. In the later stages of release (after 24 h.), the remaining drug is uniformly released to avoid secondary infections, resulting in a total release of 93 % of the drug within 96 h. This shows that the drug release behavior in hydrogels can be adjusted to enhance antimicrobial activity while avoiding the negative effects of explosive drug release [46], promote the development of engineered hydrogels [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial testing of double gradient hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: A convenient approach to developing antibacterial packaging is to incorporate active fillers in the polymer matrix through covalent and non-covalent interaction. Similarly, CD and antibiotic (AMK) were incorporated into the PCHH-n hydrogel network. Drug release behavior is affected by the degradation of the pH-responsive PCHH-n hydrogel, which resulting in differences in antibacterial ability. Contact antibacterial experiments showed that CD and AMK could achieve rapid bactericidal ability in a short time (10 min). Even at low concentration of AMK (PCHH-3) in the bottom layer, it displays high antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. 5A). Furthermore, Fig. 5B also demonstrates the antibacterial properties of the hydrogel patch through in vitro antibacterial experiments. Compared with the blank control group (Fig. 5A), the inhibition zone that hydrogels with different drug distribution have excellent antibacterial ability. which has similar antibacterial activity against S. aureus. While for E. coli, with the increase of the AMK amount of the bottom layer hydrogels, the diameter of the inhibition zone increases slightly.

We speculate that gram-positive bacteria have simple-structured cell walls, consisting of an interconnected peptidoglycan layer. The negatively charged teichoic acids on the peptidoglycan layer provide many anionic sites, facilitating the electrostatic binding of cationic molecules or nanoparticles to the bacterial cell surface. Therefore, S. aureus, gram-positive bacteria, is more sensitive to lower concentrations of cationic antibacterial molecules. After CD and AMK binding to bacteria, CD disturb the fluidity of cell membrane and enhance the permeability and retention ability of AMK inside the S. aureus [48]. While for Gram-negative bacteria, the protective outer cell membrane is rich in lipopolysaccharides (LPS), which is cross-bridged by divalent cations that act as a barrier to cationic antibacterial molecules entry [49]. Such a unique structure and composition of the outer membrane makes the membrane more impermeable to cationic antibacterial materials, resulting in weak antibacterial activity of CD and AMK for E. coli at lower drug concentrations. Therefore, compared with PCHH-n (n = 3, 4, 5), PCHH-1 and PCHH-2 with lower drug loading presentation a stronger bactericidal effect on S. aureus (Fig. 5B).

From Fig. 6A, the vitro antibacterial experiments showed that the diameter of inhibition zone experiment of the PCHH-3 hydrogels is about 30 mm against E. coli and S. aureus, which further proved that the excellent antibacterial activity of CD and AMK. The combined antibacterial activity of CD and amikacin in the hydrogels was further verified by co-culturing S. aureus and E. coli with PCHH-n hydrogel. The antibacterial activity of the PCHH-n hydrogel was shown in Fig. 6B. The bactericidal experiment proved that the hydrogel has excellent bactericidal activity with 99.5 % bactericidal efficiency within 10 min. Further, the morphology of bacteria was observed by Fig. 6C and D, the bactericidal process may be attributed to destroying the integrity of the bacterial cell membrane, so that the content of the bacteria flow out to accelerate bacterial death. Amikacin, as an aminoglycoside antibiotic, can play a bactericidal role by affecting the synthesis of bacterial proteins and thus affect the proliferation of bacteria, but antibiotics will be interfered by the defense mechanism of bacteria and reduce their antibacterial activity [50]. The synergistic antibacterial activity of CD and AMK can significantly reduce the dose of antibiotics. Cationic CD interferes with the fluidity and integrity of bacterial cell membrane, improves the permeability and retention of AMK in bacteria, inhibits or interferes with the synthesis of proteins in bacterial cells (Fig. 6E). Therefore, the synergistic effect of CD and AMK can improve the bactericidal activity, reduce AMR. The PCHH-n antibacterial hydrogel obtained by changing the physical crosslinking structure of the material itself is conducive to the uniform release of drugs, thereby improve the effective utilization of drugs. Fig. S8 shows the cell viability of PCHH hydrogels group maintained a high viability of above 95 % which was higher than the traditionally-made hydrogels group. Based on these results, it can be concluded that the PCHH-3 hydrogels possess better biosafety than traditional-made hydrogels.

The strategy of combating drug-resistant bacteria by the synergistic antibacterial effect of antibiotics and a CD matrix hydrogel is more desirable than increasing the antibiotic dosage or waiting for the development of new antibiotics. Blindly increasing the dose of antibiotics and seeking new antibiotics will accelerate drug resistance progress. The synergistic antibacterial effect of the cationic carbon dots and the hydrophilic aminoglycosides antibiotic, amikacin, against S. aureus (Gram-positive) was further investigated (Fig. 7A). From Fig. 7B, it can be found the fractional inhibitory concentrations index (FICI) is significantly reduced in the presence of a low dose of amikacin (Table S3) which CD increased the susceptibility of S. aureus to aminoglycoside antibiotics. The FICI for S. aureus was 0.37 < 0.5, indicating the potential synergistic antibacterial effect between amikacin and carbon dots.

Continuous use of antibiotics can drive the resistance of resistant pathogens to antibiotics. Furthermore, uncontrolled release of antibiotics not only increased bacterial resistance but also limited the long-term effectiveness. To investigate the level of AMR development induced by antibiotic, S. aureus was incubated with a sublethal dose of free amikacin, the combination of carbon dots (1 Mb) and amikacin (0.5 Ma) for 18 passages (Fig. 7C). In the free amikacin treated group, the MIC gradually increased from passage 4 and reached 32-fold after 18 passages, suggesting the high resistance of S. aureus to free amikacin. By contrast, the combination of carbon dots and amikacin only led to mild and delayed resistance which exhibited only a two-fold increase in MIC after 18 passages, suggesting a low risk of developing resistance. Furthermore, the effects of free amikacin group and the combination group on S. aureus growth were measured by growth curves.

As shown in Fig. 7D and E, the inhibitory effects of the two group on S. aureus are dose dependent and the growth of S. aureus was remarkably inhibited. There was scarcely any bacterial growth observed after incubation with the amikacin (1 MICa) or the combination group (carbon dots:1 MICB, amikacin: 1 MICA) at their minimum inhibitory concentration. According to the results, the PCHH-n hydrogel exhibited extreme effectiveness against S. aureus. CD co-delivery with amikacin could achieve a significantly better antibacterial activity than when AMK was used alone. These results indicated that the addition of CD could reduce the dosage of amikacin used against bacteria without influencing the therapeutic effect. In generally, the hydrogel can be degraded layer by layer under pH response, improving the sustained release behavior of carbon dots (CD) and amikacin (AMK) and enhancing the synergistic antibacterial effect.

In conclusion, we have successfully constructed engineered hydrogels with pore size gradients and drug concentration gradients using MMCE devices. The preparation process involved rapidly mixing of raw materials and in-situ gelation, providing a simple method to prepare antibacterial hydrogels with controllable drug release behavior. Regulating the dual-gradient structure within the hydrogel can improve its swelling behavior and customize drug release. The dual-gradient hydrogel gradually disintegrates and releases CD and AMK in a bacterial infection microenvironment. In vitro antibacterial experiments have shown that PCHH-n hydrogels can rapidly and effectively combat E. coli and S. aureus. The hydrogel demonstrates excellent synergistic antibacterial activity, enhances bacterial susceptibility to antibiotics, reduces antibiotic usage, and inhibits the emergence of antibiotic resistance. Furthermore, the method of preparing engineering hydrogels through extrusion printing has introduced a universal, convenient, and efficient strategy and technology for preparing multifunctional hydrogels.

Ge Bai: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chunhua Niu: Investigation. Lixia Lang: Investigation. Xuexue Liang: Investigation. Wanjun Gu: Investigation. Zhong Wei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Kai Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Klemen Bohinc: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xuhong Guo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Start-Up Foundation for High-level Professionals of Shihezi University (RCZK2021B07) and the Tianshan Talent Training Program (2022TSYCCX0115).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.compositesa.2024.108497.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
